mnemonics.title: Five Golden Signals
mnemonics.content: LATES - Latency, Availability, Traffic, Errors, Saturation (remember as 'LATES' like running late!)

mnemonics.title: Monitoring Types
mnemonics.content: SAN - System, Application, Network (cover all IT layers)

mnemonics.title: Key Metrics
mnemonics.content: UREA - Utilization, Response time, Errors, Availability (what to monitor daily)

mnemonics.title: Capacity Planning Steps
mnemonics.content: MAPE - Measure current state, Analyze trends, Plan resources, Execute changes

acronyms.usage: Tracking application transactions, response times, and user experience
Capturing actual user interactions and real-world performance data
Quantitative measure of service quality (latency, error rate)
Contractual commitment with customer for service levels
Average time to restore failed system to operational status
Average time between system failures indicating reliability
Quantifiable measure for evaluating success in meeting objectives
Plugin for executing checks on remote Linux/Unix systems
Protocol for network monitoring and management

visualTips.visualization: Draw a layered pyramid: Bottom = Infrastructure (servers, network), Middle = Application layer, Top = User experience. Arrows flow up showing data collection, alerts flow down.
Imagine a thermometer with green (normal), yellow (warning), and red (critical) zones. Alerts trigger when entering yellow/red zones.
Line graph showing utilization over time with upward trend line. Draw dotted line projecting when it hits 100% capacity.
Traffic = water flow in pipe, Saturation = water reaching pipe capacity, Latency = speed of water, Errors = water leaks, Availability = pipe working/not working

analogies.analogy: Like a car dashboard showing speed, fuel, temperature - gives real-time status of all critical components at a glance
Like your regular body temperature - when it deviates, you know something is wrong. Baseline is normal system behavior.
Like a smoke detector - triggers alarm at certain smoke level (threshold), not when just a tiny bit of smoke appears.
Like a test dummy in crash tests - simulates real scenario without risking actual users.
Like a highway at 100% capacity - cars (requests) slow down, some don't move at all (timeouts).
